comment_content:
I live in a neighborhood where kids are always outside playing — sometimes even near my car — and it genuinely brings a smile to my face every time. Their laughter and energy keep the community alive and remind us what childhood is meant to be — carefree, joyful, and full of imagination.

I just watched The Perfect Neighbor on Netflix, and that woman hated kids for no reason — the complete opposite of how I feel. If I had the money and resources, I would’ve bought that miserable house she lived in, torn it down, and built a playground in its place — a space dedicated to the children, and in honor of the mother who lost her life in the story.

Who knows… maybe one day we can actually make it happen. If we started a GoFundMe and the house includes the land, that would make the dream even more possible. Kids deserve happiness, safety, and a place to just be kids — and a playground like that would give them exactly that.

comment_content: I just saw this and was in tears. I wish there was a preventative law enforcement team within the sheriff's office when it comes to cases like these. If there is someone calling 911 so consistently over the same matter that should be a red flag. They should be automatically cited to show up in court for doing so. So many of her calls were not emergencies. Also I hope this brings awareness to many about how injunctions can help in these cases. You can file an injuction in your local courthouse and it will be reviewed and ruled on by a judge within 24 hours. If it gets granted the defendant would be served and the injunction will take effect as soon as its served by an officer. Many would say a paper cant prevent a tragedy but this wouldve been a good attempt to descalate a conflict that when on for so long it took a life.

comment_content:
Although the event described in the documentary seems perfectly aligned with the controversial concepts in American criminal law of the "Castle Doctrine" and "Stand Your Ground," the outcome of the trial determined that it was not considered self-defense.

In a similar case (James Douglas Rayl) a young man named James shows up at his girlfriend's family's door. The father fires 3-4 shots through the door. The young man is hit by the first bullet and, while turning around and fleeing, is shot twice more in the back. In other words, he is riddled with bullets front and back at a moment when there is no longer an ongoing attack. However, unlike the woman in Mükemmel Komşu ("The Perfect Neighbor"), the father who shot James is found not guilty.

The woman in The Perfect Neighbor:

1. Was home alone, whereas in the James case, there were two other people in the house.
2. Was not a strong, able-bodied man like the father who shot James.
3. Had her door being constantly pounded, indicating an ongoing attack, and she had no knowledge of whether the person at her door had a weapon or a similar object; they had previously thrown signs, skateboards, etc., at each other.
4. First called the police, saying, "They are at my door, I'm very scared." In fact, in her subsequent statement, she testified, "I was afraid you wouldn't come again, as there were previous situations where I called and you didn't come."
5. Two minutes after this call, she fires a single, blind shot through the door, killing the Black woman.

"Stand Your Ground" and the "Castle Doctrine" have critical details, such as the requirement that the attack must be ongoing and the absence of an intent for revenge. The reason the woman received a 25-year sentence was the conclusion that she acted with the intent of revenge.

However, it seems that when the father who pulled the trigger in the James case fired multiple shots through the door at his daughter's boyfriend, whom he knew had been harming his daughter for a long time, he is not considered to have acted with the intent of revenge. Yet, the woman in The Perfect Neighbor, who got into a verbal argument with the neighborhood kids, is deemed to have killed their mother with the intent of revenge over a trivial matter :)

On the other hand, the fact that the woman in The Perfect Neighbor first called the police for help and then fired a single shot through the door two minutes later supports the idea that she felt she had "no other choice." In the James case, however, the father continuing to fire at the fleeing James in the back, after the attack had ended, transforms the action from "defense" into an act of "ensuring absolute death."

In the documentary, I expected evidence to be presented showing that the woman had studied the aforementioned self-defense principles in detail on her phone or home computer and had planned to use the gun with a scenario that would justify her actions. However, there was no information in this direction.

The prosecution had to prove that the woman committed this act not as "self-defense" but as a premeditated act of "revenge" or "punishment." To support this claim, evidence should have been presented that the woman had researched "self-defense" laws in detail on her phone or computer before the incident or had planned to use the gun. Although the documentary provided no information about the existence of such evidence, through commentary, the impression was created that she acted with such intent.

In conclusion, in those distant lands where these events took place, it is not the rules of law but jury dynamics and societal prejudices that completely steer the trials. In the James case, there was the figure of a "heroic father" who killed someone he perceived as a threat to his property, saving his precious daughter from the wrath of an angry boyfriend, and he was set free. In contrast, the woman in The Perfect Neighbor was a "grumpy single woman" who killed a "mother of four" and received a 25-year sentence. Thus, it is clear and obvious to what extent the sympathy and antipathy created by the media influence the courts.
reply_content:
It was trial by media, unfortunately. Susan was always going to be vilified. The bodycam footage shows people mocking and ridiculing her — including police officers who clearly failed in their duty of care. Under the law, her situation involved a protected characteristic, which means her very first report should have been taken seriously and fully investigated.

Her allegations should have been referred to the CPS for review, especially given the involvement of the mothers. A dedicated case officer should have been assigned to Susan, particularly if her reports were going to be dismissed as “non-emergencies.” The street should have been flagged as a high-risk area, and her address should have been marked for regular patrols. At the very least, there should have been consistent monitoring and intelligence gathering to understand what was really going on.

An anti-social behaviour contract could have been implemented, but instead, they mocked and laughed at her — a complete failure of duty and compassion.

There’s clearly more to this story than that shallow, one-sided documentary suggested. Nothing in the body-worn footage or Susan’s emergency calls made her look incriminating. She was frightened and did exactly what we’re all told to do — she called the authorities for help.

We still don’t know how many people actually came to her house — was it just them, or were there other parents involved? Were there weapons? Did they threaten her directly? People who suffer from mental health issues can sometimes catastrophize situations, which is exactly why a proper risk assessment should have been carried out by the officers involved.

I really hope this case makes the police and local authorities take antisocial behaviour more seriously — sometimes the warning signs are all there, but they’re ignored until it’s too late.

It’s an absolute tragedy, and my heart breaks for those four children who have lost their mother. But I do feel that this situation wasn’t as black and white as it’s being portrayed. It seemed like there was ongoing antagonism toward this woman, and unfortunately, the parents enabled that behaviour.

The neighbours who were complaining about the so-called “Karen” were all friends whose kids played together — of course they were going to share the same opinion of her. If you’ve ever lived in an apartment complex or social housing, you’ll know that people rarely grow out of those old “schoolyard hierarchy” dynamics, and small issues can easily become group conflicts.

Although she was intense, I do believe there was truth to what she was saying — it seemed the kids and even some of the parents were deliberately trying to provoke a reaction from her. And when you’re dealing with that kind of disrespect day after day, it can really wear you down.

The whole “kids will be kids” excuse really got to me as well. Kids can absolutely have fun, but they also know there are boundaries — there are certain things they simply shouldn’t do. Why did they even need to go near her home in the first place, knowing it always ended badly?

And let’s be honest — a lot of the neighbours didn’t seem like the most responsible adults either. Some were clearly under the influence; one even had a beer in her hand during interviews. If that’s the example the kids were growing up around, it’s not hard to imagine where some of that behaviour came from.

I also don’t believe it was premeditated at all. You can tell just by looking at her that she doesn’t seem like someone who would seek out a fight. She probably felt intimidated by many of the neighbours and their children, and not everyone handles confrontation well — most people would be scared. It’s even possible she catastrophised the situation in the moment and thought more than one person was coming for her.

As for her looking up “stand your ground” laws, I don’t think that proves guilt. She could have been reading about other neighbourhood disputes and come across it, or even searched it after the fact to understand how much trouble she might be in.

Either way, that road and that community sounded rough — there’s more to it than we’ll ever know.

@GFY94 Susan stood out like a sore thumb because, up until that point, everyone who moved into that neighbourhood shared the same mindset, and those who didn’t were probably too scared to act or speak otherwise. Susan’s desire for a peaceful environment was not an unrealistic ask. This was pack mentality and learned behaviour, and the cycle continues. As you said, it was all easily preventable.

What really annoyed me was the failure in duty of care toward a protected characteristic such as Susan. The issues she reported about the children and their parents should have been taken seriously and reported to child protective services. Bylaw officers also have a responsibility to act. She should have been assigned a case handler, especially for what were considered non-emergency incidents. They could have flagged the road as an ASB area and conducted regular patrols.

​​​​@vidveles- all the people here commenting about the police not doing anything. You have to realize that police just can't lock you up or charge you based on whatever they feel. You saw the video, cops didn't witness anything. They also reported on stories that were contrary to one another and always inconsistent.  So, what is it that you think cops can do with that? Nothing. They deal with criminal law, a crime has to be committed and they need evidence of the crime presented to them or collected for them. When the police only receive frequent calls of the same  nature and are always hearing different versions of the truth and never bearing witness to any of it, then they can't enforce a law that they have no proof of being broken. 

The parents should have done more. I'm not excusing this lady, she is a piece of shit if you ask me, and everyone here knows who this type of individual is. She is a deranged and unstable lunatic. 

However, at the end of the day, no matter if your children are doing right by the law or wrong - and no matter if someone else is doing right or wrong by your children, you are responsible for your childrens safety and your own liberty. No matter who is right or wrong, your children have to come home at the end of the day, and so do you as a parent. These parents should have removed their children from this area of conflict as to not exacerbate the situation. And I don't mean the one lady who was killed, all parents on that street. There were a ton of kids there. They all should have stepped up. 

The parents should have also obtained more video evidence of her calling their kids names and doing all the things they reported the lady doing, 'then' the police could have done something. 

Having that said, this is an absolute tragedy that never should have happened, and this lady should be institutionalized.
